title: The AI Race Nobody Can Win
gist: This page is a Foreign Affairs podcast episode landing page (interview conducted by Dan Kurtz-Phelan) rather than a standalone essay; the accessible text is an episode teaser plus guest bio and a source list, not a transcript. Guest Sebastian Mallaby frames U.S. AI policy as an 'AI trilemma' — balancing innovation to stay ahead of China, national-security risk containment, and public anxiety — arguing current measures in Washington, Beijing, and Brussels only address it piecemeal.

insights.claim: Sebastian Mallaby frames U.S. AI policy as an 'AI trilemma': policymakers must simultaneously encourage AI innovation to maintain an advantage over China and power economic growth, guard against a national-security catastrophe, and address the concerns of an anxious and skeptical public.
insights.speaker: Sebastian Mallaby
insights.so_what: Gives a compact three-axis lens (innovation vs. security vs. public legitimacy) for judging whether any given AI policy proposal (export controls, safety rules, industrial subsidy) is balanced or lopsided.
insights.locator: Sebastian Mallaby calls this the “AI trilemma”

insights.claim: Mallaby argues that resolving the AI trilemma requires more than the piecemeal measures currently on offer in Washington, Beijing, or Brussels, implying no major AI power currently has a coherent policy that satisfies all three trilemma goals at once.
insights.speaker: Sebastian Mallaby
insights.so_what: Suggests regulatory and industrial-policy fragmentation persists across all three major AI jurisdictions — a reason not to expect near-term policy resolution in AI governance globally.
insights.quote: it requires more than the piecemeal measures currently on offer in Washington
insights.locator: he argues that it requires more than the piecemeal measures

insights.claim: Mallaby co-authored 'The AI Trilemma' essay with Sebastian Elbaum in Foreign Affairs, which supplies the analytical framework discussed in this podcast episode.
insights.speaker: Sebastian Mallaby
insights.so_what: Points to the primary-source essay for the full trilemma argument, since this page itself is only an episode teaser.
insights.locator: “The AI Trilemma” by Sebastian Elbaum and Sebastian Mallaby

insights.claim: Mallaby's new book 'The Infinity Machine' covers the founding of the AI lab DeepMind, adding to a body of work (also including 'The Power Law' on venture capital and 'The Man Who Knew' on Alan Greenspan) that gives him direct research grounding in frontier-AI institutional history.
insights.speaker: Sebastian Mallaby
insights.so_what: Signals Mallaby as a credible source for institutional/organizational detail on DeepMind specifically, useful for anyone researching Google DeepMind's founding history.
insights.locator: The Infinity Machine, an excellent new book about the founding of the AI lab DeepMind
